data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Strong Quarter, But Don't Get Too Excited Yet
data.description:
Introduction

If you followed me on here, then it's likely you know I'm an income-focused investor and have been bullish on BDCs (BIZD) my entire time here.

After selling out all of my positions earlier this year due to suspecting headwinds from declining interest rates, I've been waiting for an opportunity to get back in.

While BDCs have seen declining share prices due to lower base rates, some still trade at premiums above their net asset values. But with interest rates expected to continue declining in the coming months, I think investors may get even better entry points.

Me, personally, I'm looking for some BDCs for an all-income portfolio I'm building, and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) is a contender. BXSL recently reported a strong Q3, with their bottom line increasing from the prior quarter.

While this strengthens their dividend profile temporarily, investors should be wary of further headwinds in the near term.

In this article, I discuss BXSL's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why investors shouldn't get too excited just yet.

Previous Hold Thesis

I last covered Blackstone Secured Lending back in August in an article titled: I Say Chances Of A Dividend Cut Are About 50/50.

During their Q2, BXSL saw their dividend coverage fall to 100% as their bottom line decreased $0.06 due to increased competition, macro uncertainty, lower investment commitments, and lower base rates.

Their top line also retracted quarter-over-quarter but still managed to increase 5.4% from the prior year. Despite this, BXSL's fundamentals remained strong, evidenced by their low non-accruals, manageable leverage, and robust liquidity.

Due to a high potential to see further rate cuts, I suspected BXSL could potentially reduce its dividend, resulting in further downside. Since, the stock is down close to 12% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up over 4%.

Best Quarter Since Q4'24

Blackstone Secured Lending reported their Q3 earnings and saw their share price rally as a result. This was due to the BDC seeing their strongest quarter since Q4'24.

Deployments in the quarter crossed $1 billion due to increased investment activity, a 90% increase from the previous quarter. And resulted in a 15% year-over-year increase in the portfolio to $13.8 billion.

This added 22 borrowers while exiting six, bringing their total borrower count to 311 from 252 a year ago.

Blackstone Secured Lending's quarter was rather impressive, seeing how net investment income managed to increase despite lower base rates causing their portfolio yield to decline 20 basis points from Q2. This fell 120 basis points from 11.2% in the prior year's quarter, an explanation for the declining dividend coverage.

NII grew from $0.77 to $0.82, giving BXSL solid dividend coverage of 106%. However, this was still down from $0.91 the prior year's quarter, a result of declining interest rates. Total investment income climbed from $345 million to $359 million, and grew 4.7% year-over-year.

Strong Credit Quality

BXSL's latest quarter further showed why they're one of the most fundamentally-sound BDCs in the sector. Most of their commitments in Q3 were weighted towards first-lien loans, bringing their total exposure to 97.5%. This was slightly down from 98.7% last year's quarter.

Still, for comparison purposes, BXSL still had one of the most defensively-positioned portfolios amongst its peers:

Ares Capital (ARCC): 61%

Hercules Capital (HTGC): 90.5%

Capital Southwest (CSWC): 89.7%

This is also likely the reason for BXSL's low non-performing borrowers. Non-accruals were 0.1% on a cost and fair value basis, one of the lowest in the sector. This was even lower than HTGC's, whose non-accruals were 1.2% at cost and 1.1% at fair value.

Despite the strong quarter, Blackstone Secured Lending did see some markdowns, which resulted in $0.16 in unrealized losses.

This also impacted NAV, resulting in a decline from $27.33 the prior quarter to $27.15. On an annual basis their decline was slightly lower, from $27.27. This is also likely the reason for the six investment exits in Q3.

Balance Sheet Position

From a balance sheet standpoint, Blackstone Secured Lending maintains a solid position with investment-grade credit ratings from all three major agencies. Liquidity was robust with more than $2 billion available, and leverage was slightly above the peer average at 1.22x.

This increased from the prior quarter and year, but should be of no concern for investors, as BXSL just recorded their highest investment activity since last year.

However, going forward, investors should keep an eye on further increases, particularly if the economy sees further weakening, as this could indicate deteriorating credit quality.

Why Investors Shouldn't Get Too Excited Yet

Despite BXSL's strong fundamentals and the surge in investment activity, macro uncertainty still keeps me cautious.

And this is evidenced by the uptick in job losses. As a result of this and the potential to see delayed impacts from the recent government shutdown, we could see further economic weakening.

Moreover, this would also increase the likelihood of seeing more rate cuts. If so, this will result in lower yields on investments and, ultimately, in tighter dividend coverage.

I've been adamant about BDCs potentially seeing more dividend cuts in the coming months and think the sector may see further downside by Q1'26. My cautiousness on BXSL has less to do with their fundamentals and more to do with macro uncertainty and management's willingness to reduce the dividend.

Many BDC management teams have touched on potential cuts. Going forward, I, personally, think we could see more cuts in the next 3 to 6 months. While this would likely cause further share price weakness, this would still give BDCs attractive high starting yields.

Currently, the market is pricing in lower rate cut odds, down from 65% previously. But most still expect an additional rate cut next month. As a result, BDCs will see lower portfolio yields. Particularly BXSL, since they have 98.6% exposure to floating-rate debt.

This is significantly higher than ARCC's 71% and higher than CSWC's 96.3% & HTGC's 97.8%. Meaning BXSL's bottom line is more sensitive to interest rate cuts.

Going forward, investment activity could continue to increase. Especially, if the Fed can manage a soft landing. But I suggest waiting to add to any BDC, at least until the first quarter, to see how further rate cuts shake out.

Slight Discount To NAV Is Attractive

Currently, Blackstone Secured Lending trades at a slight discount to NAV with a P/NAV ratio of 0.99x. Income-oriented investors may find this attractive. Especially, since the stock is down more than 17% year-to-date.

And some peers, like ARCC, CSWC, & HTGC, all still trade at premiums above their NAVs. But the potential to see further share price weakness in the sector due to rate cuts is likely in the next 3 to 6 months.

Bottom Line

If you're an income-focused investor, adding to BXSL at a slight discount to NAV is not bad. Especially, if you plan to hold long-term. However, with economic uncertainty remaining high and the likelihood of seeing further volatility in the sector due to more rate cuts, I suggest waiting, as there could be more share price weakness. Therefore, I'm reiterating my hold for Blackstone Secured Lending.

data.title: Powell Industries' Growth Slowed In 2025; Here's Why It May Pick Up Again In 2026
data.description:
On Tuesday, Powell Industries (POWL) issued their fourth-quarter earnings. To anyone paying attention to them, it would come as no surprise they beat estimates, as they topped EPS estimates every time for the past two years.

I initiated my coverage of Powell in October of last year as a buy, arguing in favor of the solid balance sheet and the company's ability to improve their already strong margins. That rating was certainly warranted, as you can see from their performance.

Here we are, with FY2025's numbers in and the stock trading at a significantly higher price, so it's probably a good time to take another close look at them to see if the momentum is likely to slow down or if Powell continues to do everything right as they enter fiscal 2026.

We'll be looking closely at the numbers and also at potential avenues for growth in 2026, as the revenue growth in the past year wasn't as high as we'd seen in previous years. There are some reasons to be hopeful that 2025 was somewhat an off-year for revenue, despite their successes on other fronts.

Margins Continue to Improve, Revenue Growth Slows

A lot of my past praise for Powell was how much they had improved their margins in 2024, something that's not easy to do when a company is also hugely increasing revenues. Those margins hadn't peaked though, as we can see here.

2023

2024

2025

Revenue

$699 million

$1.01 billion

$1.10 billion

Gross Profit

$148 million

$273 million

$324 million

Gross Margin

21.2%

27.0%

29.5%

Operating Income

$62.5 million

$179 million

$218 million

Operating Margin

8.9%

17.7%

19.8%

Net Income

$54.5 million

$150 million

$181 million

GAAP EPS

$4.59

$12.51

$14.98

(Source: 10-K from 2024 from SEC and press release for 2025 numbers)

While revenue growth was a lot less than what we've seen in the past, Powell was able to once again blow away earnings estimates because they managed to once again squeeze a higher margin out of their business. FY2025 earnings give us a P/E ratio of 21.47, which is somewhat better than the sector median of 23.52. While the lack of top number growth may be somewhat disconcerting on the surface, there are reasons for it and reasons to think that growth in 2026 may look different from what we had here.

What Grew and What Didn't

Powell is continuing to see strong growth in certain segments of their business, as they broke down in the press release from their earnings. The biggest growth in Q4, by far, came in the Electric Utility segment, up fully 100% year-over-year. The Light Rail Power segment was also strong at 85%. Those were big growers, and you'd think, given that, the overall business would've seen higher revenue growth than it did.

Not everything was doing so well, though. Oil and Gas, historically Powell's core segment, was down 10%, while Petrochemical was down 25% and other general Commercial was down 9%. That's a substantial drag overall, particularly the Oil and Gas number, which was down 3% for the full year and 10% in the Q4.

The good news is that weakness in Oil and Gas, understandable as prices have dropped, may be temporary, as Powell reports their outlook going forward for that segment is positive, with the expectation that more investment will be made in liquefied natural gas (LNG), driving new business for them.

Light Rail Power has been strong all year and should remain so into 2026, and Electric Utility business continues to be a major focus of Powell's growth. If Oil and Gas recovers like Powell expects it to, there is reason to be hopeful that 2026 revenue growth could be higher than 2025's was.

Remsdaq and Jacintoport: Powell Invests in Growth

Historically, Powell has aimed for organic growth, but as the company grows in size and their balance sheet continues to get stronger, meaningful acquisitions become more possible for them.

We saw that this summer, with Powell's acquisition of UK-based Remsdaq Ltd. The deal, which closed this quarter, was purely a cash transaction, costing Powell only about $16.3 million. Understandably, a $16.3 million acquisition for a company doing $1.1 billion per year in sales isn't an immediate game-changer, but Powell is optimistic about its potential.

Remsdaq makes terminals for electric substation controls and subsequently fits neatly into Powell's overall goal to grow their Electric Utility business. Better still, the company says, this will allow Powell to compete better in the electrical automation business, which just happens to be one of their highest-margin segments. The hope is that Remsdaq will allow Powell to continue to grow that part of their business and also improve their margins going forward.

Anticipating growth elsewhere and a recovery of the Oil and Gas segment, Powell also announced in August they will be making a $12.4 million investment in expanding their Jacintoport facility in Houston. The goal is to make the facility more ready to support the LNG business demand as it emerges. The expansion is expected to be finished in the second half of 2026.

These two investments amount to $28.7 million but certainly aren't hurting Powell's balance sheet.

Then

Now

Cash and Equivalents

$332 million

$476 million

Total Current Assets

$736 million

$932 million

Total Current Liabilities

$418 million

$446 million

Total Shareholder Equity

$437 million

$641 million

Current Ratio

1.76

2.09

price/book ratio

6.67

6.06

(Source: press release from 2025 earnings)

Powell has ample operating capital and actually a surprisingly large war chest, which would allow them to make much larger investments should the opportunity present itself or potentially raise dividends substantially more than they are now. The dividends at present, 26.75¢ per quarter, amount to only a 0.33% yield, much lower than the sector median of 1.52%, but not outrageous for a company that still has its sights set on expanding.

Macro Risks: Could the Economy Derail the Growth?

The biggest risk, as I see it, is not internal to Powell but the economy in general. Powell is facilitating industrial expansion in general, and if the economy weakens, their customers may scale back their CapEx and subsequently scale back how much business they do with Powell.

Current investment in AI and the soaring demand for electricity should ensure that the Electric Utility segment will remain a strong grower for Powell, and I agree with their decision to prioritize it, but if the economy weakens enough, we could easily see light rail investment or planned expansion in Oil and Gas slow. We've already seen Capex weakness drag down the Petrochemical segment, and that's likely to continue into 2026 from all indications.

Though Powell remains financially secure, they need to continue at least modest growth to convince the market there is further upside to the business and to subsequently justify the high premium to book and low dividend yield. Earnings alone might not be enough unless Powell continues to trend upward, and there are some macroeconomic environments in which that would be quite challenging.

There's Still Room for Growth Here, But Prices Are Higher

In the end, I'm going to reiterate my buy rating from last year, but with expectations a little more tempered and closer to hold. Powell has room to appreciate from here if things go right, and things have definitely gone right for a long time. At higher prices, though, there is less room for modest results.

In my mind, Powell needs to keep beating estimates on earnings, to continue to grow its revenue at a respectable rate, and ideally to keep improving their margins as much as they can. While we saw 2025 margins better than 2024, the rate of growth does seem to be slowing, and further growth there is likely to have to come from more heavily leaning into higher-margin businesses, which Powell does seem to be doing.

If we see LNG projects getting put off or serious macroeconomic concerns growing, Powell might not be as appealing in the near term. They seem to be growing in the most responsible way imaginable, but investors should also appreciate that when prices are too high, consistent earnings beats are almost obligatory and less and less impressive to the market.

data.title: Salesforce's AI Upside Is Real - But Not Enough For A Buy
data.description:
In a year where mega caps have had a stellar run, with markets lapping up tech names with the slightest mention of AI, Salesforce (CRM) has seen over a 25% decline in share prices. AI-powered CRM enhancements and even a strategic acquisition of Informatica in the works do not seem to help move the dial on the ticker. The biggest drag on Salesforce is a perceived threat to its core offering. The transition towards an AI-first CRM platform, traveling through an AI-enhanced phase, will need to take out a lot of execution risks before markets overlook this perceived threat. Investments and initial AI green shoots may also not be enough.

There are reasons why the markets are asking for more proof. The core CRM play is one of the most susceptible in the new AI world. Workflows and automation of routine and repetitive tasks seem a lower hurdle for AI to replicate with greater gains. Competition is also hot in the segment. Salesforce's own AI improvements are not yet showing any re-acceleration of topline traction or incremental margin strength or defense. ServiceNow, with partial overlap in workflow automation, has also had a rough 2025, acknowledging the AI threats to the automation product offerings space. It doesn't help that Salesforce's growth has been muted anyway, leading up to this new era of AI threat.

My Salesforce Hold thesis acknowledges these threats that even a few beats and AI traction data have been unable to overturn (in terms of investor enthusiasm). The fact is that current growth-profitability numbers are still strong. In other segments, the kind of AI progress Salesforce has made would have been rewarded as early green shoots or directional signals. However, Salesforce does not seem to have much room to regain a growth or AI-narrative premium at a time when threats weigh on its core offering. As I will show in my analysis, current prices bake in the growth and margin profile reasonably well. Any value Buy perceptions, due to valuation contractions, are misplaced given structural AI threats and moderating growth. I feel Salesforce is fairly valued at the moment.

Salesforce's current positioning, however, makes it an attractive hedge play with optionality in a larger AI portfolio. Given the lack of AI premiums now, it either will rise as the AI optionality actually delivers strong and sustained gains, along with the rest of the AI portfolio. If the AI threat is being overestimated, then Salesforce will hold up better than a portfolio where AI premiums are already baked in. Overall, I see Salesforce a strong Hold for optional upsides or a hedge against AI bubbles—not a Buy until the threats are addressed meaningfully.

Rule of the Forties

Despite all the pessimism, Salesforce's growth rates are not broken yet. It has clocked as steady a revenue growth rate as can be seen in a mature SaaS company. Not spectacular growth, but certainly steady and stable. The average growth rate in the past couple of years has been in the vicinity of 9% on average. In fact, growth has re-accelerated in Q2 2026.

The free cash flow margins have improved, in keeping with Salesforce's maturing growth profile. Current free cash flow margins are around 31-32%. The free cash flow margin and growth rate combined tip the total to just over 40—the holy grail of the SaaS elite category.

In fact, in what seems a rerating trigger of its own, revenue guidance for the full year has been revised upward to $41.2b at the midpoint (~9% growth YoY for 2026). FCF guidance outpaces even the revenue growth at 12-13%. But even a healthy guidance and earnings beat streak has not been able to lift Salesforce meaningfully.

What DCF Assumes and Fair Value

I ran a DCF model to understand the level of pessimism built into current share prices. Are markets ignoring the strong growth margin profile? Results show a fair value of ~$240 embeds an ~8.5% growth rate, which is not far from the guided near-double-digit levels. The WACC rate is assumed as 10%, reasonably balanced at a time when AI threats should acknowledge risks more aggressively. The free cash flow margin also follows a growth path from 32% to 34% over the next 5 years (very close to current levels plus baking in some growth).

Fair values can rise by 10-20% at best if I assign greater growth rates, bump up FCF growth, or assign a lower WACC, but the point is current valuations do not reflect any AI-related threat pessimism. It only removes an AI premium or growth premium that could have been assigned if those factors aligned well.

The AI Play

Since current valuations do not envision any AI-related margin or topline expansion, Salesforce is uniquely placed in an AI portfolio. In case the AI bubble deflates and the actual threat to Salesforce's core offering gets deferred or altered in magnitude, Salesforce should outperform the markets not only because it does not assign any AI premium but because one of its core threats weakens.

On the other hand, if the AI play warms up to its initial traction, Salesforce could regain a narrative premium that is missing now. And that probability is quite high from what I see in the numbers reported. Data Cloud and AI ARR, though still at a small base, grew 120% YoY in Q2 2026. Deals and paid customer data show healthy trends since the launch of Agentforce. Management was very bullish about AI adoption in the earnings call.

More than 40% of our data cloud and Agentforce bookings this quarter came from existing customers expanding their investment. Agentic AI and data make the capabilities of our unified platform, the information, the logic and the workflows, more important and valuable than ever before.

The impact of Informatica is also expected to be positive. That is not built into the DCF and revenue accretion potential. Salesforce’s acquisition of Informatica helps by creating a cleaner, more connected view of customer data from multiple sources in real time. This unified data foundation makes Salesforce's AI offering smarter and more reliable because it works with accurate and complete information. Overall, the AI upside looks decent—something that makes the upside optionality very probable at some point.

Outlook and a Hold Thesis

Since the markets are ignoring the optionality and current strength in margins and growth, it may not react anytime soon to headline number changes. In Q3, I expect Salesforce to deliver another solid quarter—largely in line with guidance, with stable growth and steady FCF strength. But given the current narrative overhang, I do not expect these results to unlock a valuation rerating or meaningfully shift investor sentiment unless there is a clear acceleration in core CRM demand or a material uplift in consolidated growth from AI products.

Overall, despite the AI optionality and recent stock underperformance, Salesforce is not an outright Buy because the stock is effectively valued for its current 8-10% growth and 30%+ FCF margin profile, while the AI threat to its core workflow automation and CRM offering remains unresolved. Until either the threat dissipates or AI monetization proves durable enough to expand growth or margins, the risk-reward skews neutral. This is why Salesforce remains a Hold—its optional AI upside and lack of AI premium make it a useful hedge in an AI-heavy portfolio, but not compelling enough on standalone fundamentals to justify a Buy today.

data.title: Top American Metals Companies To Consider For 2026
data.description:
The trade war is accelerating a U.S. rare-earth revival and other countries are following suit—these are some of the top companies to watch as we head into 2026.

How North American Metals Companies are Facing a Trade War

Rare earths and strategic metals are the invisible backbone of modern life. They help power technology such as EV motors, wind turbines, smartphones, MRI machines, and advanced defense systems. Rare earth elements possess unique properties like magnetic strength, heat resistance, and light transmission. These are not easily substituted, making them essential in sectors where performance is critical.

Amid an ongoing trade war between the United States and China, the United States is focused on reviving its supply chains, creating a durable investable theme and new opportunities outside of China-based companies.

The Rare Earth Opportunity Set is Expanding Beyond China

China has long dominated mining, refining, and magnet manufacturing. In 2025, Beijing expanded export controls on rare earths, processing tech, and even extraterritorial rules for products containing Chinese-origin materials. That tightened the screws on global buyers and galvanized U.S. industrial policy, widening the opportunity for domestic projects and suppliers. And while Washington has wielded tariffs—up to triple-digit levels on select Chinese goods—as a negotiating lever, both countries have recently struck a narrow, near-term accommodation aimed at stabilizing rare-earth flows and reducing immediate supply shock. But even this temporary easing doesn’t change the strategic long-term trajectory: the U.S. is still pushing to onshore critical mineral supply chains. Federal funding, long-term offtakes, and permitting priority continue to channel toward projects that can deliver not just ore but finished inputs like magnets and battery-grade lithium—signaling that reshoring remains a long-game initiative, regardless of short-term diplomatic calm.

Other nations are following suit with governments and corporations mobilizing to build rare earth and strategic metals supply chains outside China. Several notable, recent announcements highlight the size, scope, and global coordination of that effort:

Country Organization / Lead Entity 2025 Headline Investment / Project Announcement
U.S. Department of Defense US$400m preferred-equity investment in MP Materials to build rare earth supply
U.S. Apple US$500m multi-year offtake commitment with MP Materials
E.U. European Commission Expected €22.5b covering 47 mining/refining projects across 13 member states
Australia National Reconstruction Fund AU$200m equity stake in Arafura’s Nolans rare earth mine and refinery
India National Critical Mineral Mission State geological survey tasked with identifying 1,200 exploration projects
Japan JOGMEC €110m equity/debt for a rare earth refining facility in France
U.K. CirculaREEconomy £11m grant for building UK magnet-recycling chain
South Korea Supply Chain Stabilization Fund ₩50b per year fund for public-private overseas mine stakes and stockpiles

Source: IEA, Reuters. Data as of August 2025.

Top North American Metals Companies to Keep an Eye On

As this dynamic plays out, these domestic companies are among those well-positioned to benefit:

MP Materials (MP)

MP Materials is America's only fully integrated rare earth producer, with operations spanning mining, processing, and the manufacturing of rare earth magnets. The company owns and operates the Mountain Pass mine in California, which is one of the world's richest rare earth deposits. With more than $500 million in funding and offtake agreements from both the Department of Defense and Apple, MP is scaling magnet production and downstream processing.

Lithium Americas (LAC)

Lithium Americas is a Canadian company focused on developing the Thacker Pass lithium mine in Nevada, which is one of the largest known lithium deposits in the U.S. The company is working with partners like General Motors to produce battery-quality lithium carbonate from the site to support North America's sustainable energy transition. Recent developments include the U.S. government taking an equity stake in the company as part of a restructured loan agreement.

Albemarle (ALB)

Albemarle Corporation is a global specialty chemicals company that produces critical ingredients for a wide range of products, with a focus on lithium, bromine, and catalysts. It is one of the world's largest lithium producers, with its products used in energy storage (like electric vehicle batteries), and also provides chemicals for electronics, pharmaceuticals, construction, and transportation.

Top Metals Companies to Watch Outside of China

As more countries across the world look to onshore supply of critical natural resources, these companies outside of China are among those well-positioned to benefit:

Lynas Rare Earths (LYC)(OTCPK:LYSCF)(OTCPK:LYSDY)

Lynas Rare Earths is the largest non-Chinese producer of separated rare earth materials, operating the Mt Weld mine in Western Australia—one of the highest-grade rare earth deposits in the world. The company also runs a processing facility in Malaysia and is building a U.S.-based separation plant to support Western supply chain diversification. Lynas supplies essential materials for EV motors, wind turbines, and advanced manufacturing, positioning itself as a key partner for governments seeking to reduce reliance on China.

Pilbara Minerals (PLS)(OTCPK:PILBF)

Pilbara Minerals is a leading Australian lithium producer and the owner of the world-class Pilgangoora lithium-tantalum project, one of the largest hard-rock lithium deposits globally. The company provides spodumene concentrate to major battery and chemical producers and is expanding downstream conversion capacity through joint ventures. With strong production growth, long-term contracts, and a balance sheet fortified by high-cycle lithium prices, Pilbara plays a central role in securing lithium supply for the global energy transition.

Iluka Resources (ILU)(OTCPK:ILKAF)(OTCPK:ILKAY)

Iluka Resources is a mineral sands producer and a key emerging supplier of rare earth oxides through its Eneabba project in Western Australia. Historically known for zircon and titanium minerals, Iluka is now developing one of the world’s only fully integrated rare earth refinery systems outside China. Backed by a strategic financing package from the Australian government, the Eneabba refinery will produce separated rare earth oxides essential for permanent magnets used in defense, EVs, and clean energy technologies.

What to Consider When Investing in Rare Earth Metals Companies

Investing in American rare earths isn’t like buying another software stock. It’s closer to funding a moonshot factory—part policy project, part heavy-industry build, part commodity cycle. The upside is real, but so are the moving parts. Here’s how to think about the risks.

Policy & Geopolitics: The invisible hand on the tiller

In this space, headlines can change cash flows. Export controls, tariffs, and U.S. policy decisions don’t just shape narratives—they alter project timelines, financing costs, and long-term offtakes. It’s important for investors to watch the policy calendar as closely as earnings season and track China’s counter-moves alongside U.S. incentives.

Commodity & Project Risk: Models meet metallurgy

Rare earth and lithium prices swing—sometimes violently—and projects swing with them. Even great deposits face real-world friction: permitting delays, capex creep, commissioning hiccups, and lower-than-modeled recovery rates (especially in first-of-a-kind U.S. facilities).

Supply Chain Depth: Where the margin really lives

The most attractive margins accrue as materials move from ore to metal to alloys to finished components (magnets, cathodes) and finally into OEM contracts. Accordingly, investors must consider how companies control things like processing, conversion, and locked-in offtakes with autos/tech/defense—it’s not just about a company’s pure rock-in-the-ground exposure.

2026 Outlook

Markets have already started to price the tension of the ongoing trade war, with magnet materials like neodymium and tungsten seeing significant price increases due to tighter supply. While some of those price gains have diminished following the near-term agreement between the United States and China, the longer-term trajectory of onshoring supply of these critical natural resources remains intact. Because the bigger story heading into 2026 isn’t price—it’s policy. China’s dominance in this space was built through decades of centralized state strategy, with the country consolidating mines, subsidizing refining, and allowing less stringent environmental standards. And China has shown a willingness to use its position strategically, tightening export restrictions when geopolitics heat up. For the U.S. and its allies, access to these materials is no longer just an economic question. It’s a matter of national security and energy independence. As a result, the investment momentum behind rare earths and strategic metals companies outside of China is accelerating. They’re no longer a niche materials trade—they’ve become a strategic lever in the evolving economic world order and a compelling opportunity for 2026 and beyond.

How to Invest

Directly purchasing rare earths isn’t feasible for most investors—these materials are not traded on traditional commodity exchanges. That’s where REMX comes in.

The VanEck Rare Earth and Strategic Metals ETF (REMX) provides pure-play, comprehensive, global exposure with holdings generating at least 50% of revenues from rare earths and strategic metals.

Important Disclosures

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the speaker(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

Holdings will vary for the REMX ETF and its corresponding Index. For a complete list of holdings in the ETF, please click here.

An investment in the Fund may be subject to risks which include, but are not limited to, risks related to investments in rare earth and strategic metals companies, basic materials sector, regulatory action and changes in governments, special risk considerations of investing in Australian, Asian, Chinese, and Canadian issuers, Stock Connect, foreign securities, emerging market issuers, foreign currency, depositary receipts, small- and medium-capitalization companies, cash transactions, equity securities, market, operational, index tracking, authorized participant concentration, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, passive management, fund shares trading, premium/discount, liquidity of fund shares, issuer-specific changes, non-diversified and index-related concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. Emerging market issuers and foreign securities may be subject to securities markets, political and economic, investment and repatriation restrictions, different rules and regulations, less publicly available financial information, foreign currency and exchange rates, operational and settlement, and corporate and securities laws risks. Small- and medium-capitalization companies may be subject to elevated risks. Investments in Chinese issuers may entail additional risks that include, among others, lack of liquidity and price volatility, currency devaluations and exchange rate fluctuations, intervention by the Chinese government, nationalization or expropriation, limitations on the use of brokers, and trade limitations.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of the Funds carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contain this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HIVE Digital: 'Cheap' Despite Explosive Revenue Growth
data.description:
In the 2 months since I last covered HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE) for Seeking Alpha, it has been a wild ride for shareholders, to say the least. Following a massive 100% rally in the stock from the publishing of that article to mid-October, HIVE has almost entirely retraced that rally and is now up roughly 5% since early September:

While HIVE is still generally trailing other companies in the sector, judging by the performance in the CoinShares Bitcoin Mining ETF (WGMI), it is well ahead of Bitcoin over these last 2 months. With the company having just released its latest earnings report, it's time to reassess if there is still a reason to be bullish on HIVE.

Q2 FY26 Earnings

For the quarter ended September, HIVE reported $87.3 million in top-line revenue. This was an explosive quarter with year-over-year growth of 285% and sequential growth of 91.3%:

Quarter Ended 9/30/24 6/30/25 9/30/25 YoY QoQ
Revenue From Mining $20,765 $40,797 $82,073 295.2% 101.2%
Revenue From HPC $1,883 $4,814 $5,180 175.1% 7.6%
Total Revenue $22,648 $45,611 $87,253 285.3% 91.3%

Source: HIVE Digital, Revenue in 000s

While the percentage growth for HPC revenue was impressive at 175%, it was dwarfed by the growth in revenue from mining in both aggregate and percentage terms. At $5.2 million in revenue from high-performance compute, HIVE's HPC segment made up a little under 6% of the company's total revenue in the quarter.

While 6% is still a larger percentage of HPC revenue than most of the other Bitcoin (BTC-USD) miners-turned-HPC providers in the public markets, it's actually the lowest level of HPC revenue share for HIVE Digital in the last 6 quarters. You can see in the chart above, HIVE Digital had previously grown HPC revenue as a share of total revenue for 5 consecutive quarters. Even for a company that has indeed grown revenue from HPC, HIVE is still a Bitcoin mining company if the top line is any indication. Thus, it's likely problematic that during a quarter when Bitcoin's price was never lower than it is as of article submission (November 18th, 2025), HIVE Digital still couldn't generate a positive net income:

During the quarter ended September, HIVE reported a $15.8 million net loss after taxes. But to get a better sense of the actual health of the underlying business in the quarter, we can strip out things like gains/losses on investments, digital asset holdings, and derivatives:

Revenue $87,253
Operating and maintenance costs -$44,065
High-performance computing service fees -$784
Depreciation -$38,292
Selling, general and administrative expenses -$7,800
Foreign exchange gain $601
Stock-based compensation -$5,472
Adjusted income/loss -$8,559

Source: HIVE Digital, Revenue in 000s

Even if we took out the $5.5 million in stock-based compensation, HIVE Digital still lost over $3 million in the quarter. Some of this is likely due to HIVE accelerating its ASIC depreciation as part of its Paraguay expansion. But I still don't think it gets any easier from here, given what is happening to the price of Bitcoin and the impact those price declines are having on mining Hashprice:

As of article submission, mining profitability just hit a new 52-week low of $37.5 per PH/s. More than a 52-week low, though, this is both a halving epoch low and an all-time low in mining profitability. I think this is critically important for shareholders to understand because it highlights why so many miners are pivoting to HPC. I recognize that I've made these points before. But we now have public miners who are leaving the business entirely. This should theoretically limit the growth of global exahash.

Yet so far, it's the smaller companies that are leaving mining entirely. The bigger players are often accelerating their hash growth. For instance, HIVE is guiding for 25 EH/s by the end of November. For a company that had just 5 EH/s this time last year, scaling this production capacity has been incredible to watch. The problem is, it hasn't been a good enough reason to own HIVE stock.

Balance Sheet and Dilution

Where I'll give HIVE some credit is here; they've seemingly put what was a 2,800 BTC stack at the beginning of the year to good use in 2025. At the end of September, HIVE reported holding just 210 BTC and $22.6 million in cash/equivalents. To the degree that it has been able to do so, HIVE has utilized its Bitcoin holdings to fund its capex growth:

At the end of September, HIVE had $693 million in total assets against just $68.4 million in total liabilities. With shareholder equity of just under $625 million and 228 million shares outstanding, HIVE had a book value of $2.74 per share at the end of the quarter. It should be noted that HIVE's losses have generally been funded by shareholder dilution:

Over the last 5 years, HIVE shares outstanding grew by 243%. This is substantially lower than many of HIVE's sector peers, and it's important to point that out. But still, dilution should be an expectation when holding this stock for longer than a quarter or two. From where I sit, the dilution is forgivable when it is done strategically. Which is why I find it interesting that the company wasn't tapping the ATM aggressively when the stock traded above $5 for several weeks between early October and early November.

Per the company's recent quarterly filing, HIVE issued an additional 3.6 million shares subsequent to the end of September, but just 523k of those shares came from the ATM. If HIVE needed to tap the ATM again before the end of the calendar year 2025, it feels like the company missed an opportunity to raise capital at a heightened share price last month, with the stock now trading well below $4 per share.

Valuation

Through my work on Seeking Alpha in recent years, I've made it no mystery that I don't generally like Bitcoin miners, fundamentally speaking. But buying these stocks for swing trades is something that does appeal to me in certain scenarios. I like buying the stocks when I think they're trading at a discount to peers if I feel the discount is unwarranted.

At a price-to-book ratio of 1.3, a price-to-sales ratio of 3.2, and a Seeking Alpha valuation grade of "A-," I think you could argue HIVE is cheap relative to peers. Especially considering I'd put HIVE closer to TeraWulf (WULF) or IREN Limited (IREN) as true peers given its dwindling BTC stack and HPC footprint. HIVE has prioritized scaling over HODLing like TeraWulf and IREN have, yet it trades at multiples more similar to CleanSpark (CLSK) or Riot Platforms (RIOT). The question is whether or not HIVE is undervalued or if WULF and IREN are overvalued—I'm currently leaning towards the latter.

Closing Takeaways

HIVE is not a ticker that I think has properly benefited from AI hype like some of its peers. Which could work to HIVE's advantage if the AI mania in markets starts to unwind, as it shouldn't fall nearly as far as IREN or WULF based on valuations. It could also work to HIVE's advantage if Wall Street's AI mania continues since the stock could rally higher as the company monetizes its GPUs for HPC. Execution is certainly important in these names. And I think the apparent lack of ATM usage in October was a missed opportunity to raise capital. Mining profitability broadly remains putrid, so even if you decide to take a flier on the company, I wouldn't personally fall in love with HIVE as long as more than 90% of revenue comes from mining. Having said all this, there are worse ways to play a potential dead cat rally in Bitcoin than HIVE. But since I think we've seen the highs in Bitcoin's price for this halving cycle, I'm going to downgrade the stock to a 'hold' at this time.

data.title: PHK: High Premiums, Low Spreads
data.description:
The Thesis

PIMCO High Income Fund (PHK) is a closed-end fund (CEF) that aims to generate an annual return of around 12%, paid monthly, by investing in the riskiest tranche of credit globally. When you invest in this vehicle, you are buying a global high-yield portfolio actively managed by the PIMCO management team. This portfolio is designed to carry a level of leverage that will achieve the high returns sought. For an income-oriented investor, the appeal of this portfolio is obvious, as you receive a very high coupon without having to select any securities. On the other hand, for an investor with a controlled risk profile, this CEF will not fit their risk parameters a priori.

My current thesis is to keep the vehicle in your portfolio if you are an investor focused on recurring income. The income an investor receives today is justified by the credit risk they are assuming, and the premium over NAV is not at high levels (it always has a premium). As we will see later, the increase in the term premium on Treasuries and the limited room for manoeuvre in high-yield spreads mean that, in my view, this is not the best time to increase your position in a vehicle of this type. For me, it makes sense to continue collecting the coupon if you are already invested and to keep an eye on the dividend while waiting to find better entry points in environments of premium compression.

The Fund Overview

As mentioned above, PHK is a closed-end fund with fixed capital and a flexible mandate. It does not replicate any index, which allows the management team to move between the different segments of global credit and thus achieve highly customised curve and duration management.

In terms of costs, PHK ranks in the upper-middle range for this type of product. The management fee is 0.76%, and the total expense ratio, excluding interest, rises slightly to 0.79%. In other words, for active management and the structure of the vehicle, you pay around 0.8% per annum. When we include the cost of the financing used to leverage the portfolio, the total expense ratio rises to 1.44%. This extra cost reflects how much it costs you to play with leverage. At this point, you are not only paying for PIMCO to select bonds and manage risk, you are also paying interest on the debt used by the fund to try to amplify the carry. The upside of this is that with a dividend yield of 12%, the costs do not eat into your returns, leaving an attractive margin for an income-oriented investor. The downside is that this level of expenses, added to the premium on the NAV that the fund maintains almost permanently, forces you to be very careful about investing in it. It is also one of the reasons why I do not recommend buying it at the present time.

The Holdings

Looking at the Holdings section, we find holdings that are quite ‘PIMCO style’. This means that the bulk of the portfolio is, as we know, in corporate bonds, which represent just over 60% of the total. It is in this ‘area’ where most of the coupons offered by the vehicle are generated.

We can see that the fund's effective duration adjusted for leverage is 3.6 years. This factor indicates that, at present, it does not have a high sensitivity to changes in interest rates. It is mainly affected by variations in the 3-7 year section of the yield curve. Geographically, the fund concentrates most of its bond holdings in the United States, which accounts for 78% of the portfolio. If we look at duration-adjusted exposure, this percentage rises to 80%. The rest of the geographies act as complementary pieces to the exposure in the US at this time. With this picture, we can say that the fund is exposed to the credit cycle and US interest rates (with little sensitivity to duration), with the spread differential and term premium being relevant to our analysis, as we justify below.

In terms of sectors, we can see that the CEF is heavily weighted towards real estate and finance. Real estate accounts for around 50% and finance for just over 35%, with communications as the third largest sector and energy practically irrelevant. In terms of risk, this means that the fund is highly exposed to businesses that are sensitive to the economic cycle and financing costs.

Beyond the “Pimco Fds” block (just over 9%, which is usually cash or internal vehicles), the rest of the fund's holdings are very specific high-yield and syndicated loan names. Companies such as AmSurg, US Renal, AGFC Capital Trust, Syniverse, PRISA, and Ecopetrol are businesses with medium- to high-risk profiles and attractive coupons. Even so, the top 10 only account for about 28% of the total, so the concentration risk by issuer in this top 10 is relatively low.

Dividends & Premium

As mentioned above and as evidenced by the chart above, the fund offers double-digit returns. We can see how, in recent years, the TTM yield has almost always ranged between 10% and 14%. In periods when the markets were not favourable (2020 or 2022), the return barely fell below 9%, which reinforces the consistency in terms of distribution, which is interesting for an income-oriented investor.

The ‘Dividend Yield History’ table reinforces this argument. As can be seen, since 2015, there has not been a single year of annual closing returns below 9%. This means that investors who weathered the volatility have been rewarded with very attractive returns for their patience.

With regard to the premium, we reaffirm the comment made in the thesis, where we refer to the fact that the fund operates with a structural premium in the long term. In the last year, specifically in April 2025, there was a significant contraction in the premium, largely caused by the general decline in financial markets triggered by the Trump administration's tariff policy. That situation would have been the perfect time to enter the vehicle without paying the extra amount that you would now have to pay to enter.

The Macro Overlay

In terms of spread compression, we show the change in the 21-day z-score for the different credit spreads in the US market. As can be seen in the chart, warmer colours are beginning to appear in the riskier credit tranches (BB, CCC). This means that some issuers are already beginning to notice the tightening of financial conditions, caused in part by the increase in the term premium. Given this situation, it is advisable to remain cautious and monitor the evolution of these spreads before investing in a vehicle that is so exposed to US credit risk.

In the current term premium chart, we can see a slight increase in this variable in recent times. This has a direct influence on the valuations of companies whose debt is held by PHK, as the discount rate used to update these valuations (cash flow) is increased by the term premium. It also makes long-term treasury bonds more attractive, which may lead to rotations in fixed income portfolios from credit bonds to treasury bonds. Thank you for reading.

data.title: China Gold Market Update: October's Unseasonable Strength
data.description:
Highlights

  • Gold capped further gains in October. The LBMA Gold Price PM and the Shanghai Benchmark Gold Price PM (SHAUPM) rose 4.9% and 5.5%, respectively, in the month
  • Wholesale gold demand defied seasonal patterns, rising both m/m and y/y to 124t in October
  • Chinese gold ETFs added the notable amount of RMB32bn (+US$4.5bn, +34t) last month, and gold futures volumes surged at the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE)
  • The People’s Bank of China (PBoC) has reported gold purchases 12 months in a row, adding 0.9t in October, lifting the total to 2,304t, 8% of China’s foreign exchange reserves.

Looking ahead

  • The recent Chinese gold market value-added tax (VAT) change is likely to put pressure on local gold jewellery demand as the sector is impacted by additional tax. But consumer sensitivity to price may also be lessening as the gold price has been rising steadily for more than three years now
  • The VAT change does not apply to gold bars sold by SGE members, gold ETFs or gold accumulation plans (GAPs)1. And there may be further room for growth in gold bar sales, as consumers may purchase them for jewellery making purposes.

A divided but positive month for gold

For gold, October was a tale of two halves. The metal initially soared, setting successive records on various risks and strong ETF buying, before cooling later in the month as geopolitical concerns eased and profit-taking emerged. Ultimately, both the LBMA Gold Price PM and SHAUPM finished the month positive (Chart 1), extending their stellar y-t-d gains to 44% and 42%, respectively.

Chart 1: Global gold prices ended October with further gains

Monthly returns of the SHAUPM in RMB and LBMA Gold Price PM in USD*

Wholesale gold demand defied seasonal weakness

Gold withdrawals from the SGE totalled 124t in October, 6t higher m/m and 17t higher y/y (Chart 2). This is almost on a par with the ten-year average of 127t. Investment demand improved further, especially in the first half of October when US-China trade tensions flared up, momentum in local equities cooled and the gold price surged, and this supported wholesale gold demand. Although gold jewellery consumption was robust during the early October nine-day National Day + Mid-Autumn Day holiday,2 retailers remained cautious in restocking amid the amplified gold price volatility earlier in the month.

Chart 2: Against seasonality, wholesale gold demand rose further in October

Monthly gold withdrawals from the SGE*

Chinese gold ETFs saw notable inflows in October, adding RMB32bn (US$4.5bn) in the month, the strongest since April. Their total AUM, supported by the hefty inflow and a higher gold price, jumped 24% to RMB210bn (US$29bn) whilst holdings surged 33t to 227t – both at their month-end peaks (Chart 3).

Earlier in the month, geopolitical risks and equity market weaknesses drove Chinese investors toward the safe haven of gold ETFs. But trade risks lessened after President Trump met President Xi in Korea, and the Fed’s hawkish cut tempered further bets of easing. And in response to lower safe-haven demand and a weakening gold price, local investor interest in gold ETFs faded.

Investor interest in gold ETFs revived in early November, supported by local equity pullbacks, a rebounding gold price and rising geopolitical tensions.

Chart 3: Chinese gold ETFs saw notable demand in October

Monthly Chinese gold ETF demand and month-end holdings*

Chart 4: Gold futures trading activity surged in October

Daily average trading volumes of SHFE gold futures and monthly gold price volatility*

The PBoC reported its 12th consecutive monthly gold addition to its reserves in October, adding 0.9t (Chart 5). Continual purchasing so far in 2025 has pushed China’s official gold holdings to 2,304.5t, 24t higher than at the end of 2024. Meanwhile, gold’s share of China’s foreign exchange reserves has increased from 5.5% to 8%.

Chart 5: China’s official gold reserves have risen 12 months in a row

The PBoC’s reported gold holdings and their share of total foreign exchange reserves*

China’s net gold imports amounted to 93t in September; data from China Customs shows that imports were 5t higher m/m and 36t higher y/y (Chart 6). This is consistent with the pattern we saw from September’s wholesale demand – the month’s gold withdrawals rebounded both m/m and y/y.

Chart 6: Gold imports rebounded in September

Net 7108 gold imports under various regimes*

1While these products are not impacted by the VAT reform, product providers may adjust their specific policies such as threshold of trading.

2See: Chinatax

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Iamgold: Marginal But Mighty When Gold Soars (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since I last covered IAMGOLD (NYSE:IAG) back in April, shares have nearly doubled.

Gold Stalling After Showing Signs Of Renewed Momentum

Gold started last week on a strong note, showing promising momentum on the 1-month chart as it broke through resistance near $4,150 and pushed toward $4,340. But the rally quickly lost steam by Friday. XAU slipped back below that same support-turned-resistance level and is now trading under the 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving average yet again—with the 50-day crossing below the 200-day in a classic "death cross."

The 6-month chart shows that XAU is holding just above the 20-day moving average (in purple). If XAU breaks below the 20-day moving average, it is likely to test the 50-day moving average again (in green).

Realised volatility (1-month realised volatility compared to the 1-year average) looks to be fading as well—therefore I expect a period of consolidation before XAU finally picks a direction again.

I am still bullish on gold over the long run. Central banks—especially China—still seem far from where they ultimately want their gold allocations to be. China holds less than 8% of reserves in gold, compared with a global average of closer to 20%, which suggests to me that there is more room for continued central bank buying.

Q3'2025 Results

IAMGOLD recently reported Q3'25 Earnings in the first week of November.

Revenues rose 61% in Q3 to $707 million—thanks mostly to higher gold prices as well as Côté ramping up.

It was a strong quarter for production, with IAG putting out 190k ounces of gold, taking the year-to-date total to 524k ounces. Costs did rise a bit faster than investors would like, something management acknowledged, noting:

we understand the importance of implementing diligent cost controls particularly ahead of the next stage of organic growth.

Management also announced that IAG would be initiating a share buyback program for up to 10% of outstanding common shares. This move to return value to shareholders during a period of strong gold prices mirrors similar actions taken by other gold miners recently. While ideally, you would want companies to be buying back shares when they are most undervalued, I remain cautiously optimistic that the rally in IAG still has legs if gold approaches my target price of 5k by the end of next year. However, looking ahead, I would personally prefer a greater emphasis on returning cash via dividends, as this allows investors the flexibility to decide how best to deploy their capital—but that is merely my own preference.

Operations

Côté Gold (Ontario, Canada)

Côté (which poured first gold late in March last year) had a record Q3'25, Producing 75k gold ounces attributable to IAG, up 83% year-on-year, bringing year-to-date production to 193k ounces and keeping it on track to hit annual guidance of 250k ounces-280k ounces on an attributable basis. Mining and mill throughput were strong, with recoveries reaching a record 94% following the plant hitting full nameplate capacity in June.

Even with strong production, the quarter saw Côté’s first full maintenance shutdown, during which key equipment—like the high-pressure grinding roll and ball mill—was serviced, and the primary crusher upgraded. To keep operations running smoothly, a temporary crusher was brought in, while a secondary crusher is on track for commissioning in Q4. Once online, I expect that the additional crusher will increase capacity and help reduce maintenance-related downtime going forward.

AISC for the nine months to September came in at an impressive $1,613 per ounce sold—making Côté the lowest-cost producer among all of IAG's assets.

The Gosselin zone (which is in close proximity to Côté) looks to be shaping up as an important growth area for IAG. Drilling efforts this year have focused on confirming the size and quality of this resource and upgrading a portion of lower-confidence mineral estimates into higher-confidence categories. The program was initially planned for 45k metres of drilling, but has already expanded—with 18.5k metres drilling taking place in the third quarter, and 50k metres having taken place year-to-date. Anther 6.5k metres of drilling is planned to explore promising areas nearby—with those results expected to feed into an updated resource report by the middle of next year, which will give investors valuable visibility.

Westwood Complex (Quebec, Canada)

Westwood's gold production hit a rough patch in Q3'25. Output came in at 23k gold ounces—a 28% drop from the same period last year—bringing year-to-date output to 76k ounces. At this pace, full-year production for this asset looks set to come in below IAG's guidance range of 125koz to 140koz (with management admitting in Q3 earnings that they are unlikely to reach even the lower end of that guidance range).

Mining activity, however, was anything but slow in Q3. The crews moved 405k tonnes of ore in the quarter, up a whopping 88% from last year, driven largely by a very busy Grand Duc open pit. Underground mining, which minded 90k tonnes of ore, came out at 6.42 g/t—but here is where the challenges kicked in. A change in the mining sequence, tougher than expected ground conditions, and a bit more dilution than planned meant lower grades than the company had hoped. The good news to investors is that the IAG management team seems to be on top of it as they have been tweaking blasting techniques and refining stope designs. These actions have already started paying off. Underground ore in October average over 9 g/t.

Management remarked in Q3 Earnings that:

Underground mining rates are planned at 1,000 tpd from multiple active mining zones, while grade is expected to increase in the fourth quarter of 2025 as the mining activities sequence through stopes in higher grade zones and implement improved blasting controls. Open pit activities from Grand Duc are currently being evaluated for an expansion and extension of the pit, given the elevated gold price, with a decision to be made in the fourth quarter of 2025.

AISC for Q3 were severely impacted by all of this, and came in at $2,793 per ounce.

Essakane (Burkina Faso)

Essakane's gold production hit 92k ounces in the third quarter, down 8% from the same period last year—and brings year-to-date production to 255k ounces. IAMGOLD's stake in Essakane dropped from 90% to 85%, after Burkina Faso's government "took" an extra 5% stake—courtesy of the new mining code rolled out in August 2024 under the beret-wearing leadership of Ibrahim Traoré.

Full-year attributable production for Essakane is therefore expected to land at the low end of the guided 360k-400k ounces range.

Earlier the year in April, the Burkina Faso government also increase gold royalties. Previously, gold sold at or above $2,000/oz carried a 7% royalty. Under the new rules, gold priced at $3,000/oz or above is now taxed at 8%, with the rate rising by 1% for each additional $500/oz increment.

It definitely hasn't been an easy quarter for Essakane. Management also commented in Q3 Earnings on the security situation:

Security-related incidents are still occurring in the country, and more broadly, the West African region. The situation in Burkina Faso continues to pressure supply chains, and although the impact has lessened, in-country fuel shortages necessitated a temporary reduction in mining activity in the third quarter. The processing plant remained fully operational during this period. The Company continues to take proactive measures to ensure the safety and security of in-country personnel and is constantly adjusting its protocols and activity levels at the site in response to the security environment.

Exploration

In October, IAMGOLD made a bold move and announced the acquisition of Northern Superior and Orbec, consolidating a massive 134k hectare land package in the Chibougamau district. I like this move—it gives IAG one of Canada's largest pre-production gold camps and creates a real "hub" opportunity, where multiple deposits could feed a central processing facility. I think the numbers also back it up. The combined Nelligan Mining Complex now boasts 3.75Moz Measured and Indicated, and 8.65Moz Inferred resources. Nelligan itself continues to impress me. Indicated ounces jumped 56% to 3.1Moz, and Inferred ounces rose 33% to 5.2Moz this year, with some juicy drill hits also catching my attention. Monster Lake, just 15km North, also looks to be a high-grade gem—with drill results showing that the veins are consistent and strong.

In my view, IAMGOLD is building something here that could pay off over the long term—and I especially like the fact that they are doing so in the Tier 1 mining jurisdiction of Canada. The combination of scale, high-grade zones, and a potential central mill is exactly the kind of setup that gives optionality and flexibility, in my opinion.

Risks

As is clear to anyone reading this article, IAMGOLD is dealing with its fair share of challenges. Essakane is navigating security risks and fuel shortages, as well as clear jurisdictional risks. Westwood's underground operations have shown how tough underground mining can be at times. On top of that, I'm a bit weary about near-term gold prices after the strong rally over the last couple of months. Mining is never straightforward, and while I remain bullish on gold over the long term, investors should expect some bumps along the way.

Valuation

I have selected the below peers for a relative valuation comparison with IAMGOLD. I focused on mid-tier gold producers and development-stage miners with similar operational footprints, cost structures, and growth profiles.

I prefer using FWD EV/EBITDA ratios when valuing mining companies, as this tells me what I am paying for the underlying operations relative to the earnings they are forecast to generate. Over the past two years, IAMGOLD traded at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 14% below peers—and that is exactly where it is still trading at, suggesting to me that shares are fairly valued at current levels.

Interestingly, IAG (in the orange line below) has significantly outperformed almost all of the peers listed above—except New Gold, which is one of my favourite miners in the space.

Analyst Recommendations

A majority of analysts that publish on the Bloomberg Terminal have a "Buy" rating for IAG—and a consensus 12-month price target that is 19.4% above the current share price.

The Technical Take

Following the recent pullback towards the 50-day moving average, shares bounced off that level and are exhibiting strong upward momentum again. The MACD indicator is also in bullish territory. That said, I would like to see trading volumes pick up again before I can be confident that the rally has genuine strength.

Conclusion

I am upgrading IAMGOLD from Hold to Buy, and agree with the 12-month consensus price target of $16.39. This isn't necessarily because the company is materially better than peers—its quality remains below many peers in my opinion—but because I am taking a slightly more constructive approach with my ratings, evaluating the stock within the broader investable universe rather than limiting the comparison to its sector peers. Marginal, lower-quality producers like IAG can outperform quality miners when gold rallies sharply, as we have seen recently. The technicals also look enticing here—and a Hold rating may simply be too conservative. However, my Buy rating is contingent on the gold rally continuing, as IAG will likely underperform other higher quality names like AngloGold, when gold prices retreat sharply.

data.title: The Outlook For S&P 500 Dividends In November 2025
data.description:
The S&P 500's dividend outlook for the final quarter of 2025 brightened in the month since our last snapshot of their outlook.

On 15 October 2025, the CME Group's S&P 500 Quarterly Dividend Index' Futures quotes indicated investors could expect to collect $19.90 per share during the period covered by 2025-Q4 dividend futures contracts. On Friday, 14 November 2025, that rose to $19.94 per share, an increase of 0.2% from the previous month's outlook.

Our October 2025 snapshot also presented the projections for the S&P 500's dividends per share through the second quarter of 2026. Here is how the dividends expected in the future quarters of 2026-Q1 and 2026-Q2 changed in the past month:

  • 2026-Q1: Decrease of $0.01, dipping to $20.89 per share
  • 2026-Q2: Increase of $0.02, rising to $19.73 per share

For this dividend snapshot, we're extending the outlook for the S&P 500's expected dividends per share through the fourth quarter of 2026. The following chart presents the S&P 500's quarterly dividends per share as provided by dividend futures contracts from 2024-Q1 through 2026-Q4.

A note of caution on the dividend projection for 2026-Q4. Because it covers expectations that are currently more than four quarters into the future, it's best understood as an initial projection based on very thin trading at this point of time. The projection for this quarter can be expected to change once it falls within the typical year-ahead period that defines the typical time horizon for investors and the investment activity for its related dividend futures contract picks up.

More About Dividend Futures Data

How changes in the outlook for dividends at specific points of time in the future contribute to changes in stock prices is described by this math.

For this series, we have been taking a snapshot of the CME Group's S&P 500 quarterly dividend futures data shortly after the second or third week of each month.

Dividend futures indicate the amount of dividends per share to be paid out over the period covered by each quarter's dividend futures contracts, which start on the day after the preceding quarter's dividend futures contracts expire and end on the third Friday of the month ending the indicated quarter. As determined by dividend futures contracts, the now "current" quarter of 2025-Q4 began on Saturday, 20 September 2025, and will end on Friday, 19 December 2025. From the perspective of dividend futures, the next quarter of 2026-Q1 will begin on Monday, 22 December 2025.

Because dividend futures are tied to options contracts that run on this schedule, that makes these figures different from the quarterly dividends per share figures that are reported by Standard & Poor's. S&P reports the amount of dividends per share paid out during regular calendar quarters after the end of each quarter. This term mismatch accounts for the differences in dividends reported by both sources, with the biggest differences between the two typically seen in the first and fourth quarters of each year.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sweetgreen: Can't Be Saved By Millennials And Gen Z
data.description:
Sweetgreen has had a rough 2025 as the company has continued to see traffic decline which has led to a decrease in the company’s revenue. I noted several challenges in my prior article, and many of these headwinds remain ongoing.

Year to date Sweetgreen is now down over 80% which is nowhere near the S&P 500’s annual YTD return of roughly 15%.

I’m reiterating my “Hold” rating as I do like some of the moves the company is making and from a valuation perspective the stock is now very reasonable. However, there are some internal issues as well as negative macro factors. Let’s dive in and discuss macro conditions as well as the recent quarterly results.

Gen Z and Millennials Can’t Save Sweetgreen

As noted in a University of Michigan survey, consumer sentiment is at its lowest point in over three years. Much of this consumer anxiety is due to the government shutdown, which should be nearing a resolution. Still, as stated in the article, many middle class Americans are feeling financial strain. I’d say Gen Z and Millennials are no exception to that strain. According to Smart Asset, roughly 73% of all wealth in the United States is held by Americans over 55 and beyond (the Baby Boomer generation).

Given that data, it’s no surprise to see consumers like Cava (CAVA), Chipotle (CMG), and Sweetgreen (SG) struggling as these are a few Millennial and Gen Z restaurant favorites. On the company’s Q3 earnings call, new CFO Jamie McConnell stated, “So the 25 to 35 consumer is the most under pressure, and they make up about 30% of our consumer base, and they're down about 15%.”

I will say, I like that Sweetgreen’s management team stated the company is launching a “protein” campaign where patrons can select 1 of 9 menu items with more than 30 grams of protein. Management also noted that the protein portions have increased by 25% which I think is a smart move given the current macro-environment and seeing that Millennials and Gen Z consumers have frequently complained about Chipotle’s inconsistent protein portions.

Despite these seemingly positive menu moves, I still worry Sweetgreen isn’t doing enough to get new customers into the door as traffic dropped again this quarter and has now been trending in an unfavorable direction as you can see below:

These menu moves seem primarily focused on the same demographic. Unless the company begins targeting different consumers or creating lower priced value items I’m not sure this move is enough to differentiate their offerings from the competition.

Operational Efficiencies

In my last article, I mentioned Sweetgreen hired Jason Cochran as their Chief Operating Officer. At the time, Cochran believed only one-third of stores were meeting his operational standards. Cochran launched an initiative he dubbed, Project One Best Way, to create standards to establish operational efficiencies. This quarter the company launched Sweetpass, which provides a framework team members can use to get up to snuff. Cochran also created “scorecards” to give to stores to give them better visibility into company metrics and easily illustrate where stores are succeeding or faltering.

After these initiatives, Sweetgreen's management believes that now 60% of stores now meet operational standards. While these are nice sounding objectives, these really mean nothing until traffic improves and these improvements in operational metrics correlate to improvements in financial metrics.

From an operational perspective, I believe it’s worth noting that earlier this month, the company decided to sell Spyce Food Co. to Wonder Group. Spyce Food is the business unit responsible for developing the company’s Infinite Kitchens. On the earnings call, here is what CEO Jonathan Neman had to say about the sale, “Under our agreement with Wonder, Sweetgreen will continue to utilize and expand Infinite Kitchen technology across our restaurants. Partnering with Wonder enables us to leverage their manufacturing scale, R&D investments and shared innovation, accelerating the refinement and rollout of additional IK units. This transaction also allows us to sharpen our focus on our core restaurant business, allocating more of our talent and financial resources toward accelerating growth and achieving profitability. The $186.4 million sale is expected to infuse our balance sheet with approximately $100 million in liquidity, strengthening our financial position and enhancing our flexibility to fund future growth initiatives.”

I think there were some really interesting questions raised by analysts on the earnings call about this sale, as a few questioned the reasoning and timing behind the decision. Personally, I don’t think Sweetgreen would be selling their Infinite Kitchens if the company was in a position of strength. If you look at the company’s cash balance over the last few quarters you can see the business has steadily been losing more cash:

To me this deal was done to provide an influx of cash as the company plans to open 15-20 net store openings in 2026.

Insider Buying

As many of my readers will know, I'm a big fan of founder-led organizations as I believe they tend to beat the broader market over time. Recently, co-founder and CEO, Jonathan Neman purchased shares as did the company's Chief Concept Officer, Jammet Nicolas. I believe there are numerous reasons why a CEO might sell but there's only one reason to buy and that's because he or she has a bullish future outlook on the business. This roughly $1 million dollar purchase by Neman illustrates his conviction in the business and his belief he can turn things around.

Financials

Sweetgreen generated revenue of roughly $172 million in Q3 2025 which was only a slight decrease compared to Q3 2024. As I noted above, same-store sales declined by 9.5%, whereas the company saw same-store sales increase by 5.6% in the prior year’s third quarter. Both restaurant-level profit and restaurant level profit margin declined compared to prior year as Q3 2025 restaurant-level profit was $22.5 million and profit margins were 13.1% In Q3 2024 restaurant-level profit was $34.9 million and profit margins were 20.1%.

Coupled with higher restaurant operating costs and higher operating expenses this has led to an increase in net loss and net loss per share for both the 2025 three month and nine month periods as you can see below:

Sweetgreen investors continue to see more dilution as the number of weighted average shares outstanding has risen over the year.

Like last quarter, management revised their guidance downward. Management had expected to have negative same-store sales growth of -4% to -6%. Now management expects declines of -7.7% to -8.5%. Revenue is expected to be in the range of $682 to $688 million, versus $700 to $715 million in the prior guide. The company is expecting restaurant level profit margin to be around 14.5% compared to 17.5% in the prior guide and adjusted EBITDA has been revised downward to $10-$13 million for the remainder of fiscal year 2025.

While much of this isn’t promising Sweetgreen does have a decent balance sheet as you can see below with still more current assets than current liabilities:

Plus the company is getting a cash influx thanks to their recent sale.

Valuation

Sweetgreen has a valuation grade of a "D+" at Seeking Alpha:

Sweetgreen continues to remain unprofitable, so I believe price to sales is likely the best valuation metric for this business.

Even though Sweetgreen's current forward P/S ratio is higher than the sector median, if you compare the company's P/S ratio to other high-growth restaurants such as Dutch Bros (BROS) Chipotle (CMG), and CAVA (CAVA), Sweetgreen is the least expensive of the four and outside of CAVA I'd say their P/S ratio has seen the second biggest decline:

Analysts still expect Sweetgreen to deliver single to double-digit revenue growth over the next 5 years and Wall Street remains bullish on the company as the average target stock price is $8.23, implying strong upside as you can see below:

Conclusion

Outside of increases to protein sizes, there isn’t much to like about this quarter. The company has continued to see sales and traffic decline as profit margins shrink.

Sweetgreen has instituted numerous initiatives to improve operational efficiencies but I’m not sure these will prove to be worthwhile given the state of macro-economy and specifically how generations with less wealth, such as Millennials and Gen Z are facing tighter budgets.

Despite the negatives, I do love the fact Neman purchased more shares. To me this is a bullish sign. I also think Sweetgreen is less expensive compared to earlier in the year and is less expensive compared to other higher-growth restaurant companies.

However, I don’t think this will be a quick turnaround and I’d only purchase shares if I was a long term investor betting on the prospect young consumers will continue to patronize Sweetgreen in search of more health-conscious offerings.
